title: Companion Objects
description: Create static data that belongs to the class itself.

slides.title: Class vs Object (Again) ⚖️
slides.content:
## Shared Data

Normally, when you create multiple Objects from a Class blueprint, every single object gets its own completely isolated copy of the properties.
If `Car A` drives 50 miles, `Car B`'s mileage remains 0.

But what if we want to track a property that belongs to the ENTIRE blueprint collectively? Like `Car.totalCarsEverBuilt`?

slides.title: The Companion Object 👯
slides.content:
A **Companion Object** is a special block inside a class that allows you to define "static" properties and functions. 

These properties belong purely to the Class blueprint itself, NOT the individual objects generated from it.

quizData.question: What is mathematically the primary structural purpose of a Companion Object?

options.text: It allows defining properties/methods that belong directly to the Class blueprint itself globally, accessible without ever explicitly establishing an Instance externally.

quizData.explanation: Companion objects are heavily utilized to store global constants, factory methods, or tracking aggregate statistics tied directly to the overarching parent Class conceptually.

slides.title: The Kotlin Way 🛤️
slides.content:
## No 'static'

If you are coming from Java or C#, you are probably furiously searching for the `static` keyword. Kotlin entirely destroyed the `static` keyword natively in favor of Companion Objects, maintaining a purer object-oriented philosophy structurally!
